one_line_takeaway: A well-structured survey of the nascent QML security landscape that gives a black/gray/white-box taxonomy and a pipeline-stage CIA threat map, cataloging quantum-specific attacks (model extraction, transpiled-circuit RE, crosstalk side channels, pulse-level and poisoning attacks) alongside quantum-native defenses (logic locking, noise watermarking, secure partitioning, output perturbation) drawn largely from the authors' own prior work.
key_finding: The central synthesized claim is that classical adversarial-ML threats do not transfer trivially to QML but re-manifest in quantum-specific forms, and that several intrinsically quantum properties (NISQ noise signatures, circuit reversibility, crosstalk, pulse-level analog behavior) can act as BOTH attack vectors and defense primitives. Concretely, the survey highlights that transpiled circuits alone (no training data or source code) suffice to reverse-engineer a functionally equivalent PQC; that noise can be turned into a non-invasive hardware watermark; and that model partitioning across providers plus hardware/architecture output perturbation can degrade clone fidelity. It concludes that current defenses are largely static and siloed, and that the field lacks standardized threat benchmarks and formal guarantees.
core_contribution: This is a survey / book-chapter (not an empirical paper) that organizes and synthesizes the emerging literature on adversarial threats to Quantum Machine Learning (QML) in the NISQ era. Its own contributions are conceptual rather than experimental: (1) an attacker-access taxonomy (black-box / gray-box / white-box) tying adversary profiles, target artifacts, and capabilities together (Table 1); (2) a mapping of confidentiality/integrity/availability (CIA) threats onto the stages of the QML execution pipeline (preprocessing, encoding, PQC construction, hybrid training loop, cloud deployment) in Fig. 1, distinguishing 'generic' threats (data/output theft, poisoning, DoS, ransomware) from 'quantum' threats (VQC IP theft, circuit obfuscation abuse, rerouting, latency injection); (3) a structured catalog of surveyed attacks (model extraction/counterfeiting of QNNs, crosstalk side channels, quantum-specific label poisoning, encoder-inversion in hybrid circuits, transpiled-circuit reverse engineering, pulse-level and channel-level attacks, oracle IP theft) and defenses (quantum logic locking, hardware-noise watermarking, side-channel mitigation, secure model partitioning, hardware-induced output perturbation); and (4) a set of design guidelines and future directions for building trustworthy QML systems. The numeric results in the chapter are quoted from the surveyed primary works, not produced by the authors here.
threat_model: The chapter adopts an access-level threat taxonomy specific to QMLaaS / cloud quantum deployments. Black-box: an external API client with no internal access, targeting QML outputs to perform model stealing / functionality replication (also crosstalk side channels from a co-tenant on shared NISQ hardware). Gray-box: a cloud provider or semi-privileged adversary with partial visibility (e.g., the transpiled circuit or the classical-to-quantum data encoder), enabling data poisoning via encoder state similarity and encoder-inversion to isolate the parameterized model C(theta). White-box: a transpiler/infrastructure insider or malicious cloud provider with full access to PQC internals, transpiled gates, and pulse schedules, enabling reverse engineering, Trojan/backdoor injection, oracle IP recovery, and pulse-level supply-chain attacks. Assets at risk are the confidentiality of input data and encoding schemes, the proprietary IP of variational quantum circuits, and model integrity/availability. The recurring assumption is that quantum circuits are reversible and that abstraction gaps (gate vs. pulse layer, noisy analog behavior) make manipulation stealthy.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical validation, no unified benchmark, and no head-to-head comparison of the surveyed defenses; all quantitative claims are inherited from the cited primary works and are not independently reproduced. Coverage is heavily weighted toward the authors' own line of work (Penn State group: Guardians of the qGAN, QUID, E-LoQ, QuMoS, model-stealing evaluation), which may bias the catalog. The chapter itself notes open gaps: balancing noise for reliability vs. security, cross-platform/multi-tenant leakage, entanglement-injection and gradient-hijacking attacks remaining unexamined, and the absence of standardized evaluation protocols and formal security guarantees for QML. Defenses are acknowledged to be context-specific and, in the case of HVIP/HAVIP, insufficient on their own as hardware scales.

datasets_used: No datasets of the survey's own; the following are reported from surveyed primary works
MNIST (used by surveyed HVIP/HAVIP defense evaluation)
Simulated noisy IBMQ backends quito, belem, manila (surveyed QuMoS evaluation)
quantitative_results: E-LoQ quantum logic locking (surveyed [24]): with incorrect keys, Total Variation Distance (TVD) and Hamming Variation Distance (HVD) often exceed 0.9 and Degree of Functional Corruption (DFC) approaches -1; post-decryption fidelity loss constrained below 1%; even 6-bit keys provide sufficient entropy

Hardware-noise qGAN watermarking (surveyed 'Guardians of the qGAN' [17]): CNN provenance classifier reaches near-perfect accuracy for single-hardware-trained models and approximately 90% for models trained across sequences of multiple hardware; selecting 5 backends from a pool of 15 yields a collision probability on the order of 1e-5

QuMoS secure partitioning (surveyed [22]): discovers secure configurations with accuracy typically within 1-2% of centralized NAS baselines, occasionally outperforming them; meaningful security achievable with as few as 2 providers
HVIP/HAVIP output perturbation (surveyed [18]): up to 15.71% label mismatches in Top-1 queries and 10.2% TVD in Top-k outputs; cloned models suffer up to 13% test-accuracy drop
baselines_compared: Survey performs no experiments and therefore introduces no baselines of its own

Reported within surveyed works: QuMoS compared against centralized neural architecture search (NAS) baselines and naive multi-provider partitioning; HVIP/HAVIP clone models evaluated with vs. without defense activation across PQC-1, PQC-6, PQC-17, PQC-19; E-LoQ compared against baseline (correct-key) circuit output and against earlier locking schemes requiring one qubit per key bit
